dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI won the day by being the only entity with a net positive aggregate sentiment score, despite some criticism around profitability, privacy risk, and ChatGPT fatigue [E1][E2][E3]. Praise for Codex helped keep it above water [E4], while Anthropic, Google Gemini, and Microsoft Copilot all finished net negative, with Anthropic notably taking the heaviest and broadest criticism [E5][E6][E7][E8].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI was the clearest winner on 2026-04-06 because it posted the highest score (0.072), and was the only one above zero. Its discussion was mixed rather than uniformly enthusiastic: negative threads hit on lack of profitability ahead of a possible IPO, legal/privacy concerns about data flowing to OpenAI, and mild dissatisfaction with ChatGPT’s experience [E1][E2][E3]. But unlike the others, it also had a clean positive driver in praise for Codex [E4], which was enough to lift overall sentiment into positive territory. Anthropic ranked worst by a wide margin (-0.558) and also had by far the largest volume of discussion (595 mentions), so its negative result appears broad-based rather than anecdotal. The evidence points to sustained developer frustration: criticism of company strategy and lost goodwill, blocked CLI calls, Claude Code breaking engineering workflows, and Claude feeling actively adversarial to users [E5][E6][E7][E8]. That combination made Anthropic the day’s main negative outlier. Microsoft Copilot also had a substantially negative day (-0.431), driven by complaints about poor answer quality, unfavorable or absurd-sounding terms-of-use framing, and a bloated Windows implementation [E13][E14][E15][E16]. Google Gemini landed negative too (-0.152), with complaints about AI overviews, allegedly dishonest API behavior, UX fatigue, and degraded model behavior [E9][E10][E11][E12]. Gemini’s sample was much smaller (24 mentions), so its negativity was present but less central to the day than Anthropic’s. In short: OpenAI won less because it had overwhelming praise and more because it avoided the strongly negative sentiment that dragged down the other three, especially Anthropic.

openai.judgementSnippet: OpenAI finished first and was the only net-positive entity. The conversation included real negatives around profitability, privacy/compliance risk, and ChatGPT fatigue [E1][E2][E3], but praise for Codex [E4] helped it emerge as the day’s sentiment winner.

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Anthropic had the worst day overall, with the most negative score and the largest discussion volume. Complaints centered on lost developer goodwill, blocked CLI usage, Claude Code breaking workflows, and a generally frustrating Claude experience [E5][E6][E7][E8].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Google Gemini ended net negative, driven by annoyance with AI overviews, harsh criticism of Gemini API behavior, and complaints about model/UX quality [E9][E10][E11][E12]. The signal was clearly negative, though based on a relatively small mention count.

microsoft_copilot.judgementSnippet: Microsoft Copilot also had a clearly negative day. Criticism focused on weak answer quality, embarrassing or unreliable terms-of-use positioning, and a bloated Windows product experience [E13][E14][E15][E16].

evidence.summary: The headline says OpenAI is not profitable ahead of a possible IPO, which is a negative assessment of its financial position/company strategy.

The post frames OpenAI as the destination for sensitive API keys and customer data that legal teams want to block, implying a privacy/compliance concern. The criticism is about accidental data leakage to OpenAI rather than OpenAI's model quality or pricing.
The story title frames ChatGPT as something the author is 'bored of scrolling,' which signals mild dissatisfaction with the product experience.
Directly praises Codex, which is an OpenAI product/model, with no mixed or negative language.
The story title directly criticizes Anthropic’s company strategy and says it is losing developer goodwill.
The item directly attributes blocking CLI calls to Anthropic, which is a clear negative product/UX judgment toward the provider.
The title directly criticizes Claude Code, an Anthropic product, as breaking complex engineering workflows after the February updates.
The item directly says Claude feels like it is 'actively working against me,' which is a clear negative judgment of Anthropic's product/experience.

The post includes a Gmail customization feature to 'Remove AI overviews,' indicating annoyance with Google’s AI overview feature. This is a direct negative judgment of the AI product surface, not a broader discussion of model quality.
Gemini is singled out directly and harshly: the author says "looking at you Gemini" and calls Gemini API behavior "blatant lies," saying it "pissed me off the most."
The title pairs Gemini with ChatGPT as something the author is bored of scrolling, implying mild negative sentiment about the product UX.

The commenter attributes a bad review-quality regression to 'Gemini's book,' saying it 'silently quits trying' and becomes 'sycophantic,' which is a direct negative judgment of Gemini-style model behavior.
The comment directly disparages Copilot's answer quality and calls Microsoft a laggard, which is strongly negative toward the Copilot product/surface.
Mocks Copilot’s ‘entertainment’ framing and implies Microsoft should be liable for harmful output, treating the terms-of-use wording as absurd and unfavorable to Copilot/Microsoft.
The story frames Copilot for Windows 11 as bloated and resource-heavy, highlighting that it includes a full Edge package and uses more RAM. That is a mild negative on the Copilot product experience.
The story frames Copilot’s own terms of use as saying it is only for entertainment, which reflects negatively on the product’s reliability/usefulness.
